title: Chapter 35: The Vow
content: The wedding is small.

That's the first thing I notice — how small it is. How it fits inside my chest like something I could carry without breaking. Twenty chairs in a stone chapel on the West Cork coast, a building so old the walls have settled into the landscape like they grew here, like the cliff decided to sprout a church just because Ireland is like that. Beautiful without trying. Sacred without permission.

The stone is cool beneath my fingertips when I touch the doorframe. Rough-hewn granite, grey and green with age, and the smell of it — rain-soaked rock and centuries of salt air — hits me like a homecoming I didn't know I was waiting for. Wildflowers crowd the path to the entrance, fuchsia and foxglove running riot in that careless Irish way, and their scent is sweet and damp and alive. The air tastes like the sea, which is right there, crashing against the rocks below the chapel yard, close enough that I can hear it breathing under everything.
Margaret is already in the front row, crying before anyone has even said anything.

She's wearing a blue hat that defies physics and a smile so wide it looks painful, and she's dabbing at her eyes with a handkerchief that's already soaked through. When she sees me lingering at the back of the chapel, she mouths something I can't quite parse — "beautiful" or "finally" or maybe both, because Margaret Gallagher has been waiting for this moment with the patience of a woman who raised a son who kept vanishing and finally, finally came home.

Mika stands beside me, shifting her weight from one combat boot to the other. The boots are scuffed and perfect and very clearly Mika, and they're paired with a suit so sharp it could cut glass — black, tailored, single-breasted, worn with the top button of the shirt undone because Mika Torres has never fully committed to anything conventional in her life. Her hair is slicked back. She looks like she could rob a bank or walk a runway, possibly simultaneously.
"You good?" she asks, and her voice is softer than usual.
"I'm good," I say, and I mean it in a way I've never meant anything.

She adjusts my train — slight, ivory, a whisper of fabric — and then checks her pocket where the rings are, and then checks her other pocket where someone's phone is buzzing, and then rolls her eyes at herself because she's Mika and she's nervous and she doesn't know what to do with that information.
The cats are not invited.
Gatsby does not know this.

He keeps appearing at the window — the narrow, arched window to the left of the altar — pressing his enormous orange face against the glass like a very furry ghost, his eyes round and unblinking, his mouth slightly open in that gormless way cats have when they've decided you're not paying enough attention to them. I can see him from where I'm standing, and I have to press my lips together to stop from laughing, because of course. Of course Gatsby is haunting my wedding. He's been haunting every important moment of my life since I moved to Clonakilty. Why stop now?
"Your cat is being a menace," Mika says.
"He's not my cat. He's his own cat."
"That's what every enabler says."

The music starts. A single fiddle — because this is Ireland, and because we are who we are — playing something old and slow and aching, the kind of tune that sounds like the sea remembering something it lost a thousand years ago. The sound fills the chapel and bounces off the stone and settles into my bones, and I think: this is it. This is the moment I walk toward something instead of away from it.
And then I see him.

Julian stands at the altar. Or the chapel equivalent — a stone archway wreathed in wildflowers, ivy and dog roses and something purple I don't know the name of, woven through the ancient rock like the building itself is celebrating. The flowers smell incredible, sweet and green and alive, mixing with the salt air and the candle wax from the tapered candles burning on the windowsills, and the combined scent is so specific and so perfect that I know I'll never smell anything like this again without being transported back to this exact moment.
He's wearing a suit.

Not Julian A's armor — that charcoal sharpness that made him look like he was going into battle. Not Julian B's linen — that effortless coastal ease that made him look like he'd already won. Just a suit. Navy blue, well-fitted, the collar open just slightly at his throat. It fits him the way clothes fit people who are comfortable inside them, who aren't using fabric to hide or to impress but simply to be present. He looks like a man who chose to stand here. Who stood up this morning, put on a suit, and thought: I am going to marry the woman I love, and I am not going anywhere.
His eyes are wet before I even start walking.

I can see it from here — the glassiness, the way he's blinking too fast, the way his jaw is doing that thing it does when he's trying very hard not to feel something and failing completely. Margaret's son. The man who disappeared for three years, who changed names and cities and left pieces of himself scattered across the world like breadcrumbs he never intended to follow back. Standing still. Waiting for me.
I start walking.

The dress moves with me — ivory, simple, a slight train that whispers against the stone floor. I found it in a vintage shop in Galway three weeks ago, crammed between a rack of beaded monstrosities and a 1980s shoulder-padded disaster, and I knew immediately. Didn't agonize. Didn't make a spreadsheet. Didn't call Mika for a second opinion or wonder if there was something better in the next shop over. I saw it, and I thought: this one, and I bought it, and I walked out of the store before the universe could suggest alternatives.
For once in my life, I didn't agonize.

The stone is cool beneath my thin-soled shoes. I can feel every ridge and ripple of eight hundred years of footsteps pressed into this floor, and I think about all the women who walked this path before me — in wool and silk and linen, toward men they chose or were chosen for, toward lives they embraced or endured. I think about how many of them were afraid. I think about how I'm not.

Mika walks beside me for a few paces, then falls into her position, and I'm alone on the aisle. Just me and the distance between us. Just me and the way he's looking at me, like I'm the answer to a question he spent years being too scared to ask.
This is what it looks like when a man chooses to stay, I think. Not because he can't leave. Because he doesn't want to.

I reach him. The fiddle trails off into silence. The chapel holds its breath, which is absurd because buildings don't breathe, but this one does, I swear it does. The stone seems to lean in, listening.
"Hi," I whisper.
"Hi," he whispers back, and his voice cracks on the word, and I watch a tear slip down his cheek, and I love him so much it rearranges my atoms.

The officiant — a woman with silver hair and a voice like warm wool — guides us through the beginning. The legal bits. The declarations. I hear them and respond and I'm present for all of it, but I'm also floating about three inches above my own body, vibrating at a frequency that feels like joy and terror and certainty all mashed together into something I don't have a word for.
And then it's time for the vows.
We wrote our own.

This was my idea, because of course it was — I'm still me, still the woman who needs to put things in her own words, still the woman who can't just borrow someone else's language for the most important promise she'll ever make. Julian agreed immediately, and I suspect he's been rewriting his for weeks, because when he speaks, every word feels like it's been placed with the precision of a man who knows the weight of language.

He takes my hands. His are warm and slightly trembling, and I feel the slight roughness of his palms — he's been working in the garden again, I can tell, there's still soil under one fingernail that Margaret definitely noticed and definitely chose not to mention — and he looks at me with those green eyes, and he begins.

"I spent three years disappearing." His voice is steady, but just barely. "Changing names, changing cities, changing everything except what I believed in. And then you appeared — literally appeared, in a café that smelled like coffee and cats — and I stopped wanting to disappear."
I feel my own eyes burning. The café. The coffee. The cats. The universe cracking open like an egg and me tumbling through the shell right into his life.
"I started wanting to be found."

He pauses. The chapel is so quiet I can hear the sea outside, the relentless breathing of the Atlantic, and somewhere a bird is singing, and inside this ancient structure a man is promising to stop hiding.
"Avery." He says my name like it's the most important word he's ever spoken. "I vow to be found. Every day. For as long as you'll have me."

The tears fall. Mine, not his — he's already cried his, and now he's smiling at me with the kind of tenderness that makes my chest cave in. I am looking at a man who spent years making himself invisible, and he is standing in front of God and his mother and my best friend and one very determined cat, and he is promising to be seen.
I take a breath. The air tastes like candle wax and wildflowers and the salt of my own tears.
"My turn," I say, and my voice wobbles, and I let it, because I'm done pretending I'm not feeling exactly what I'm feeling.

"I spent my whole life terrified of making the wrong choice." The words come out steadier than I expected. "I lived in three worlds to avoid choosing just one. I kept every door open because I was so afraid of closing one that I never walked through any of them. And then the universe made me choose — it didn't ask nicely, it just — it dropped me into a reality I didn't recognize and said: pick. Pick a life. Pick a door. Pick a world."
I can hear myself, and I can hear how true it is, every word, the whole messy architecture of my fear laid bare in this tiny chapel on the edge of the world.
"And I chose you."
My voice breaks. I let it.
"And it was the first time I ever chose something without being afraid."
Julian's hands tighten around mine. I can feel his pulse through his fingertips, or maybe that's my pulse, or maybe it's both of us, synced up, beating together.

"Julian Gallagher." I say his name and it feels like a spell, like the kind of word that makes things real. "I vow to keep choosing you — not because I have to, not because there aren't other options, but because you make me brave enough to pick one and stay."

The silence that follows is enormous. It fills the chapel like water filling a vessel, like light filling a room, like every version of me who ever existed standing still for one perfect moment and nodding in unison: yes. This. Exactly this.

The officiant smiles and says something about rings, and Mika produces them with more ceremony than strictly necessary, winking at me as she drops them into our palms. The Claddagh ring — his grandmother's, the one he proposed with — is on my finger already. The band I slid onto his is simple, warm gold, engraved on the inside with a line he showed me once in a letter: *Here and hereafter.* The ring is cool against his skin, then warm, then part of him.
The officiant pronounces us married.
And then Julian kisses me.

Not the desperate kiss from Clonakilty, when we were still figuring out how to want each other without breaking. Not the deliberate kiss in the gallery, when we were proving something to ourselves and to the world. This kiss is different. This kiss is the kind that comes after promises — rooted, sure, full of the kind of certainty that only exists on the other side of doubt. His lips are warm and familiar and mine, and I kiss him back, and it tastes like salt and promises and forever-but-not-the-static-kind, the active kind, the kind you build day by day by day.
We pull apart, and the world rushes back in.

Mika wolf-whistles — loud, piercing, the kind of sound that could shatter glass or at least make the officiant flinch — and then she's grinning so hard her face might split, and she doesn't even try to hide it.

Margaret is sobbing. Not delicate tears, not dabbed-away drops. Full, heaving sobs, the kind that shake her whole body, and her blue hat has gone slightly askew, and she's clutching her soaked handkerchief in both hands, and she looks at her son and at me and she mouths "thank you" and I lose it entirely, tears streaming down my face, laughing and crying at the same time, and Julian's arm is around me, and I can feel his chest shaking with the same hybrid emotion.
And then I notice.
Gatsby has somehow made it inside.

I don't know when. I don't know how. The window was closed — I'm sure it was closed — but Gatsby is many things and "deterred by closed windows" is not one of them. He's sitting in the front row, on the chair next to Margaret's empty one — she's standing now, still crying — and he's cleaning his paws with the calm dignity of a creature who absolutely belongs here, who has always belonged here, who never received an invitation because none was needed.

I can hear him purring. That low, resonant rumble that cats produce when they're pleased with themselves and the universe in general. It threads under the sound of Margaret's sobs and Mika's laughter and the sea outside and the fiddle starting up again, and it sounds like home.

Julian follows my gaze and bursts out laughing, and then I'm laughing, and Margaret is laughing through her tears, and even the officiant is smiling, and Gatsby continues cleaning his paws with the serene indifference of a being who has never once questioned his right to be exactly where he is.
Outside the chapel, the Irish sky does what Irish skies do.

It rains and shines simultaneously — because Ireland does not believe in weather that commits to a single emotional register — and the result is a rainbow so vivid it looks like a hack in the simulation. A great arching smear of color across the Atlantic sky, red and orange and yellow and green and blue and violet, so bright and so close it seems like you could reach up and put your hand through it, like the universe is showing off, like reality itself is celebrating.

Julian and I step outside into the half-rain, half-sun, and the rainbow is right there, enormous and impossible and undeniable, and I think: of course. Of course there's a rainbow. Of course the sky can't decide any more than I could, for most of my life, and of course it made something beautiful out of its indecision.

The rain is warm on my face. The sun is warm on my shoulders. The wildflowers are nodding in the breeze, and the sea is thundering against the cliffs, and somewhere behind me Mika is taking photos on her phone and saying something about how this is "aggressively cinematic" and she's right, it is, it's aggressively cinematic and I don't care, because sometimes life is cinematic, sometimes the film score kicks in and the lighting is perfect and the cat appears in the front row, and you just have to let it happen.

Julian's hand finds mine. Our fingers interlace. I can feel the ring on his finger, warm gold, *here and hereafter*, and I can feel my Claddagh on my own finger, heart pointing inward, meaning: taken, meaning: chosen, meaning: I am not going anywhere.
"Avery Gallagher," he says, testing the shape of it.
"Avery Chen," I correct, grinning up at him through the rain-shine. "I'm keeping my name."
"Gallagher by choice," he says, and his smile is the kind of smile that could power a small city.
"Gallagher by choice," I agree.

I am Avery Chen. I have always been Avery Chen. But I am also — by choice, by vow, by the deliberate and terrified and triumphant act of picking one path and walking it — Gallagher. Not instead of. In addition to. Two names, two lineages, two histories, braided together like the wildflowers in that stone archway, like the threads of the three lives I used to live, all converging here, on a cliff in West Cork, in the rain and the sun and the impossible rainbow.
I hold the hand of the man I chose in every world that mattered.
And I think: I used to be afraid of choosing wrong.

I used to stand at the fork and freeze, paralyzed by the geometry of possibility, terrified that every path was a loss, every choice a small death, every door closed a world I'd never get back. I used to think that the garden of forking paths was a trap — too many options, too much beauty, too much at stake.
But there's no wrong choice in a garden of forking paths.
There are only paths.
And I walked one.

And it led here — to a stone chapel by the sea, to a man with green eyes who vows to be found, to a best friend in combat boots who wolf-whistles at weddings, to a mother-in-law who cries before the ceremony starts, to a cat who refuses to be excluded from anything, to a rainbow that looks like the universe is showing off, to a name I chose and a name I keep and a life I built not by avoiding choice but by making one, bravely, terrifiedly, joyfully, over and over and over again.
The rain softens. The rainbow holds.
Gatsby presses against my ankle, purring so hard I can feel it in my bones.
And I think: this is it. This is the life I chose. This is the garden I'm growing.
And it is so, so good to be here.
